title: Potato Gratin (Gratin Dauphinois)

ingredients.name: starchy potatoes (Russet, Désirée, or any large potato)

subs.name: thinly sliced raw banana (vazhakkai) for a starchier, more robust version — unusual but delicious
subs.notes: cooking time reduces to 50 minutes total

subs.name: thick coconut milk (200 ml) + 100 ml milk — gives a dairy-free gratin with a subtle sweetness
subs.notes: reduce the milk quantity slightly; the flavour changes but the texture is similar

ingredients.name: garlic cloves
visual_checks: 2 for the cream, 1 for rubbing the dish
ingredients.notes: 2 for the cream, 1 for rubbing the dish

subs.name: Amul processed cheese slices (4–5 slices) laid on top — melt to a satisfying golden crust
subs.notes: or use Parmesan for a sharper flavour

subs.name: a bay leaf and a pinch of dried rosemary — equally aromatic in the cream infusion
subs.notes: remove the bay leaf before pouring

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 160°C. Butter a shallow ovenproof baking dish (about 25×18 cm) generously, then cut one garlic clove in half and rub the entire interior of the dish with the cut surface. Discard that garlic piece.

Peel the potatoes and slice them very thin — about 2 mm — using a sharp knife or a mandoline. Do not rinse the slices; the surface starch helps the cream thicken and bind. Stack the slices as you go and keep them dry.

Slice the remaining garlic cloves finely. Combine the cream, milk, sliced garlic, thyme sprigs, nutmeg, salt and pepper in a small saucepan. Heat over medium until the mixture just begins to steam and small bubbles appear around the edge; do not boil. Remove from heat and set aside.

Arrange the potato slices in the prepared dish in overlapping layers like tiles, seasoning lightly with salt and pepper between each layer. Pour the warm cream mixture evenly over the potatoes — it should come about three-quarters up the stack.
Scatter the grated cheese evenly over the top. Cover the dish tightly with foil and place in the preheated oven. Bake covered for 40 minutes — the potatoes will cook through in the cream.

Remove the foil, increase the oven to 200°C and bake uncovered for a further 20 minutes until the top is deeply golden and bubbling and a knife or skewer slides through the potatoes with no resistance at all. Rest 10 minutes before cutting — this allows the cream to set slightly for neater portions.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cream → thick coconut milk (200 ml) + 100 ml milk — gives a dairy-free gratin with a subtle sweetness (reduce the milk quantity slightly; the flavour changes but the texture is similar); cheese → Amul processed cheese slices (4–5 slices) laid on top — melt to a satisfying golden crust (or use Parmesan for a sharper flavour); thyme → a bay leaf and a pinch of dried rosemary — equally aromatic in the cream infusion (remove the bay leaf before pouring); potato → thinly sliced raw banana (vazhakkai) for a starchier, more robust version — unusual but delicious (cooking time reduces to 50 minutes total)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: If you have an oven, use it precisely as written — the slow low heat is what makes this dish. Without an oven, layer the potatoes in a kadai with a tight-fitting lid, pour the cream mixture over, cook on the lowest possible flame for 35–40 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender, then remove the lid and cook a further 5 minutes to concentrate. Finish by holding a hot tawa over the surface for 1–2 minutes to lightly brown the top. Fresh cream (Amul or similar) works perfectly here.
